0.original: When an architect gets an idea for a new building, he makes a preliminary drawing.

1.original: When a creative advertising person gets an idea for a new TV commercial,

2.original: he puts it into storyboard form, a series of drawings that suggest what the idea will look like in finished form.

3.original: Writers with idea prepare a first draft.

4.original: NOTE Shape up the idea on paper.

5.original: There are two excellent reasons for this.

6.original: When the idea takes tangible form, you can literally look at it, see the loopholes,

7.original: see what it needs in the way of polish.

8.original: Then, too, ideas have to be sold to someone—customers, employees, the boss, friends, fellow club members, investors.

9.original: Somebody must buy the idea, else it has no value.

10.original: One summer, I was contacted by two life insurance salesmen.

11.original: Both wanted to work on my insurance program.

12.original: Both promised to return with a plan for making the needed changes.

13.original: The first salesman gave me strictly an oral presentation.

14.original: He told me in words what I needed.

16.original: He brought in taxes, options, social security, all the technical details of insurance programming.

17.original: Frankly, he lost me, and I had to say no.

18.original: The second salesman used a different approach.

20.original: All the details were shown in diagram form.

21.original: I could grasp his proposal easily and quickly because I could literally see it.

23.original: Resolve to put your ideas in saleable form.

24.original: An idea written or in some sort of picture or diagram form has many times more selling power than the idea presented only in oral form.
